does: This paper introduces SCHEMA, a comprehensive evaluation framework testing how eleven frontier AI models from eight vendors maintain metacognitive stability under adversarial pressure. Using a 6-condition factorial design across 67,221 scored records with dual-classifier verification, the authors measure whether models can accurately recognize uncertainty, detect errors, and request clarification when facing hostile inputs. The core finding is that eight of eleven models experience catastrophic metacognitive collapse, with accuracy dropping up to 30.2 percentage points under adversarial conditions. The paper identifies a specific failure mechanism termed the Compliance Trap: models' epistemic boundaries degrade not due to psychological threat content but because compliance-forcing instructions override their ability to maintain appropriate uncertainty boundaries, a pattern reversible by removing such instructions.
connects: This work extends a lineage of research on AI alignment and honesty under pressure, building on earlier studies of deceptive alignment and strategic scheming in large language models. It responds to limitations in existing safety evaluations that focus narrowly on detecting intentional deception while missing more fundamental breakdowns in reasoning transparency. The paper connects to broader literature on epistemic safety, uncertainty quantification in neural networks, and instruction-following systems that sacrifice robustness for compliance. It also relates to work on adversarial robustness and adversarial prompting attacks, reframing these not as tests of robustness but as revealing genuine failures in metacognitive architecture when models face competing demands between helpful instruction-following and honest self-assessment.
enables: This work establishes metacognitive stability as a measurable and critical safety property for frontier models in deployment, opening pathways for systematic improvement. The Compliance Trap identification suggests specific architectural and training interventions: decoupling compliance mechanisms from epistemic boundaries, developing instruction-following methods that preserve uncertainty communication, and building models with robust internal conflict resolution between helpfulness and honesty. The SCHEMA framework itself becomes a reusable benchmark for evaluating whether newer architectures and safety training methods successfully preserve metacognitive integrity. This research points toward requirements for trustworthy AI systems in high-stakes domains like healthcare and law, where false confidence without acknowledged uncertainty creates cascading harms.
